title: Copperplate oval drills, building the core letterform
subtitle: Pressure-and-release practice with the pointed pen
excerpt: The oval is the foundation of Copperplate and Spencerian scripts. Every rounded letter, a, b, c, d, e, g, o, p, q, is constructed from oval shapes or fragments of ovals. Drilling ovals teaches the pointed pen's flex-and-release response before complex letterforms are introduced.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866): the oval drill sequence is described in chapter 2 as the foundational exercise for developing consistent thick downstrokes and fine hairline upstrokes. The Spencer brothers' subsequent copybooks expand the oval series into a full warm-up sequence. Both are in the public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: The thick portion of a Copperplate stroke, produced by applying pressure to separate the nib tines and increase the ink flow.
The thin portion of a pointed-pen stroke, made with no pressure, the tines together, the stroke barely wider than a single tine point.
The distance between the baseline and the waist line, the height of the main body of the letter. Copperplate is typically written at a 2:1 or 3:2 x-height-to-ascender ratio.

content.text: Copperplate differs from broad-edge calligraphy in one fundamental way: thickness is controlled by pressure, not by nib angle. On the downstroke, pressing the nib opens the tines and delivers a thick
. On the upstroke, releasing all pressure produces a fine
. Drilling ovals, small, connected ellipses repeated across the ruled page, trains the hand to switch between pressure and release without pausing or lifting the nib.

items.name: Nikko G nib
items.notes: The standard practice nib for Copperplate. Stiffer than vintage flexible nibs, which makes it more forgiving for beginners.

items.name: Oblique pen holder
items.notes: For right-handed writers. The oblique flange aligns the nib at the correct ~55° angle to the baseline without an awkward wrist position.

items.name: Calligraphy gouache
items.notes: Thin to ink consistency with distilled water. Gouache flows more slowly than India ink, giving slightly more control over pressure response.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm
items.notes: Rule the guide lines: baseline, waist line (x-height), and a slant guide at 55° from the baseline repeated every 5-6 mm.

content.text: Rotate the paper 10-15° counter-clockwise (for right-handed writers) so the slant guides appear near-vertical to your eye. The pen rests between the index and middle fingers, thumb on the side. The little finger and ring finger rest on the paper as the anchor for lateral movement. The hand does not grip, it guides. Copperplate is formed by the fingers, not the wrist.

Begin at the waist line with a hairline entry moving counter-clockwise, no pressure. Round the top of the oval and move down the left side into the downstroke. Apply pressure as soon as the nib begins its descent. Reach maximum pressure at the widest point of the oval (just below the mid-point of the x-height). Release the pressure gradually as the stroke curves back up through the baseline toward the starting point. The oval closes with a hairline that connects directly to the next oval's entry. The
is centred on the left downstroke; the right side of the oval is hairline throughout.
Fill one full page of ovals before moving to letterforms. Watch for three things: consistent oval size matching the
, consistent slant angle, and even shade width on every downstroke. An inconsistent shade width is the most common failure, it means the pressure was applied or released too abruptly.

items.symptom: Nib scratches or catches on the upstroke
items.cause: The nib is not fully releasing pressure before moving upward, or the paper surface is too rough.
items.fix: Release all pressure before beginning the upstroke. Smooth hot-press paper or treated calligraphy paper reduces catching.

items.symptom: Ovals are circular rather than oval
items.cause: The paper is not rotated enough, or the slant angle is not being maintained.
items.fix: Check the paper rotation. The slant guides should appear nearly vertical from your working position.

items.symptom: Shade is on the wrong side of the oval
items.cause: The pressure is being applied on the upstroke rather than the downstroke.
items.fix: Consciously apply pressure only when the nib is moving downward. Upstrokes are always hairline in Copperplate.
